FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional study from May 2020 COVID-19 survey from ABCD Study (N=5412 adolescents aged 10-14 years). Uses U.S. ABCD data.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/social media variables: multiplayer gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing internet, and streaming movies/videos/TV. All meet qualifying criteria.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses (Table 2) test associations between mental health/resiliency factors and total screen use. Mental health: B=0.29, 95% CI 0.06-0.52, P=.01; Perceived stress: B=0.67, 95% CI 0.43-0.91, P<.001; Social support: B=-0.32, 95% CI -0.59 to -0.04, P=.02; Coping: B=-0.17, 95% CI -0.26 to -0.09, P<.001.

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients, CIs, and p-values reported in Table 2 for all associations with screen use outcomes.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Cross-sectional study with inferential statistical analyses linking screen use to mental health/resiliency outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (JAMA Pediatrics, January 2022, Vol 176, No. 1). Not a preprint, conference abstract, or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes smartphone/screen use as independent variable (predictor) with mental health and resiliency factors as outcomes (cross-sectional analyses). Phone/SM is not the dependent variable; instead, mental health/resiliency are outcomes. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: All inclusion criteria clearly met. Study examines screen use associations with mental health outcomes, not sociodemographic predictors of screen use.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from ABCD Study (N=5335, ages 10-14) collected May 2020. National prospective cohort study in United States.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/social media variables: multiplayer gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, and total recreational screen time. All meet FT-IC2 criteria.

FT_IC3.evidence: Paired t-tests (Table 2) compare parent vs adolescent reports across screen time categories. Linear regression analyses (Table 4) examine sociodemographic predictors of parent-adolescent screen time differences. Multiple coefficients reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2: t-values and P values for all screen time categories. Table 4: Beta coefficients (B) with 95% CIs and P values for linear regressions (e.g., Age: B=0.20, 95% CI 0.11-0.29, P<.001).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Cross-sectional study examining parent-adolescent discrepancies in screen time reporting with inferential statistics.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Academic Pediatrics, April 2022, Vol 22, No. 3, pp 413-421). Peer-reviewed journal; not preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Although analyses include sociodemographic predictors of screen time differences, the primary analyses are cross-sectional (Table 2 paired t-tests compare parent and adolescent reports at same timepoint). Cross-sectional analyses do not trigger FT-EC7 per E14 rule. FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal/prospective analyses with demographic-only predictors of future screen use.
notes: Cross-sectional design with both parent and adolescent screen time reports. Sociodemographic variables predict discrepancies between reports, not screen use itself. FT-EC7 does not apply.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD dataset (N=9,337 individuals with baseline social media use and diffusion-weighted MRI data). Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures social media consumption as exposure variable, grouped by daily use: <30 minutes/day (8,868) vs >30 minutes/day (455). Platform-unspecified but explicitly states 'social media use' as predictor.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regressions examine effects of social media consumption on fractional anisotropy (FA) of 34 white matter tracts. Odds ratios assess demographic/physical/neighborhood influences on social media use. Multiple inferential tests reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios reported for demographic/socioeconomic associations with social media (p<0.05, Odds Ratio >1.5). Linear regressions examined FA effects, though final results after race/SES adjustment are noted as non-significant.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Observational study examining relationship between social media use and white matter structure using MRI data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Biological Psychiatry (2025, Vol 97, pp S36). Peer-reviewed journal article; not preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Study includes social media as independent variable predicting white matter outcomes (FA). Also examines sociodemographic and health predictors of social media consumption. Cross-sectional baseline design with white matter (neural) outcome. FT-EC7 does not apply because outcomes are not limited to future screen use prediction; study has individual neural/health predictors and includes screen use as IV.
notes: Study examines social media consumption in relation to brain white matter health. Sociodemographic variables predict social media use, but white matter structure is the primary outcome. Neural outcomes (white matter) present; FT-EC7 does not apply.
